trivial.example: People think play is trivial, but it is actually very important.

vitally.example: Play is vitally important for child development.

infant.definition: A very young child or baby.
infant.example: An infant learns to concentrate through play.

concentrate.definition: To focus all your attention on one thing.
concentrate.example: Playing helps a child learn to concentrate.

absorbed.definition: So interested in something that you forget about everything else.
absorbed.example: The child was completely absorbed in building with blocks.

imagination.definition: The ability to create pictures and ideas in your mind.

peers.definition: People who are the same age or at the same level as you.
peers.example: Children learn social skills by playing with their peers.

creativity.definition: The ability to make new and original things or ideas.
creativity.example: Free play builds creativity in children.

exploration.definition: The act of searching and discovering new things.
exploration.example: Play is about exploration and discovery.

respected.example: A child's play should be respected as real work.

Montessori.definition: A method of teaching developed by Maria Montessori that focuses on letting children learn by themselves.
Montessori.example: The Montessori method says you should not interrupt a focused child.

interrupted.definition: Stopped in the middle of doing something.
interrupted.example: A child who is concentrating must not be interrupted.

constructing.example: The child was constructing a story while playing.

narrative.definition: A story or account of what happened.
narrative.example: The child's play had a beginning, middle, and end — a complete narrative.

foundation.definition: The base that everything else is built on.
foundation.example: Play lays down a foundation for completing tasks.

undistracted.definition: Not having your attention pulled away.
undistracted.example: An undistracted baby can play for minutes with a simple object.

tissue.definition: A soft, thin piece of paper used for cleaning or blowing your nose.
tissue.example: The baby played happily with a piece of tissue paper.

grab.definition: To take hold of something quickly.
grab.example: The baby learned how to grab the toy.

gaze.definition: A long, steady look at something.
gaze.example: Follow your baby's gaze to see what interests them.

cliche.definition: A phrase or idea that has been used so much it is no longer interesting.
cliche.example: The cliche about children loving the box more than the toy is often true.

acquaintance.definition: Someone you know but who is not a close friend.
acquaintance.example: A two-year-old child of my acquaintance loved simple toys.

doting.definition: Loving someone very much, often too much.
doting.example: Her doting parents bought her many presents.

landfill.definition: A place where waste is buried; here meaning useless things that become rubbish.
landfill.example: Many toys end up as landfill because children don't play with them.

bricks.example: A few bricks are enough for hours of play.

dressing-up.definition: Wearing costumes and pretending to be someone else.
dressing-up.example: Dressing-up clothes help fire children's imagination.

craft.definition: An activity involving making things with your hands.
craft.example: Craft materials like paint and paper are great for kids.

overwhelmed.definition: Feeling too much of something; unable to deal with it.
overwhelmed.example: Too many choices can make children feel overwhelmed.

frozen.definition: Unable to move or act because of shock or too many options.
frozen.example: Too much choice leaves people frozen and unable to decide.

psychologist.definition: A person who studies the mind and how people think and behave.
psychologist.example: The psychologist did experiments on how people make choices.

flit.definition: To move quickly from one thing to another without staying long.
flit.example: With too many toys, children flit from one to the next.

indulged.definition: Allowed someone to have what they want, especially too much.
indulged.example: Buying too many toys is often indulged in by guilty parents.

novelty.definition: The quality of being new and unusual.
novelty.example: A child needs your attention, not the novelty of a new toy.

devise.definition: To think of and create a plan or idea.
devise.example: Children devise creative games all the time.

frustrating.definition: Making you feel annoyed because you cannot do what you want.
frustrating.example: It can be frustrating when a child demands you play.

demands.definition: Asks for something strongly, as if it must happen.
demands.example: The child demands that you join their game.

tackle.definition: To start dealing with a problem or task.
tackle.example: You can tackle your housework after playing with your child.

pile.definition: A large amount of things on top of each other.
pile.example: She had a pile of laundry to fold.

self-absorbed.definition: So focused on what you are doing that you forget about others.
self-absorbed.chinese: 全神貫注於自己的活動
self-absorbed.example: Once the child becomes self-absorbed in play, you can step back.

continually.definition: Happening again and again without stopping.
continually.example: The child will continually interrupt you if you refuse to play.

nuisance.definition: Something or someone that annoys you or causes problems.
nuisance.example: Don't make your child feel like they are a nuisance.

content.definition: Happy and satisfied with what you have.
content.example: Once the child is content, they can play on their own.

invest.definition: To put time or effort into something for future benefit.
invest.example: Invest time positively at the beginning of playtime.

bucket.definition: A round container with a handle, used for carrying things.
bucket.example: The girl asked her father to get the bucket for sand play.

tide.definition: The rise and fall of the sea level.
tide.example: They played with the wet sand where the tide had gone out.

autopilot.definition: Doing something automatically without thinking about it.
autopilot.example: The child found her inner autopilot and played independently.

leisure.definition: Free time to relax and enjoy yourself.
leisure.example: Daddy managed to get a bit of leisure while his child played.

preoccupied.definition: Thinking about something so much you cannot focus on other things.
preoccupied.example: If a child is preoccupied with worry, they cannot play freely.

fretful.example: A fretful child cannot relax enough to play.

organized.example: Children enjoy organized games like cards and cricket.

cricket.definition: A team sport played with a bat and ball, popular in Britain.
cricket.example: Playing cricket together is a fun family activity.

facilitate.definition: To make something easier to happen.
facilitate.example: Other adults can help facilitate play for children.

Monopoly.definition: A popular board game about buying property and making money.
Monopoly.example: The family brought out the Monopoly board at Christmas.

amusement.definition: A feeling of being entertained or finding something funny.
amusement.example: The game was met with amusement from the adults.

enthusiasm.definition: A strong feeling of excitement and interest.
enthusiasm.example: The children showed great enthusiasm for the game.

declaring.example: He stood up, declaring he was going to walk home.

thrive.definition: To grow well and be healthy and successful.
thrive.example: Children thrive when they have mixed-age playmates.

parallel.definition: Happening at the same time but separately, side by side.
parallel.example: Young toddlers often have parallel play rather than playing together.

sophisticated.example: Older children teach younger ones more sophisticated behaviour.

nurture.definition: To care for and help someone grow and develop.
nurture.example: Older children learn how to nurture younger ones.

cousins.definition: Children of your aunts and uncles.
cousins.example: She had the most fun playing with her cousins on holiday.

festivals.definition: Special events or celebrations, often with music and activities.
festivals.example: Children love running around at outdoor festivals.

boundaries.definition: Rules or limits that keep you safe.
boundaries.example: Children need boundaries to feel safe.

structured.definition: Organized with a clear plan and rules.
structured.example: Too many structured activities leave little time for free play.

screens.definition: Electronic devices like phones, tablets, and computers.
screens.example: Children should not spend too much time in front of screens.

addictive.definition: So enjoyable that it is hard to stop doing it.
addictive.example: Screen time can become addictive for children.

deprivation.definition: Not having something that you need.
deprivation.example: Denying screens altogether is another sort of deprivation.

caution.definition: Care taken to avoid danger or mistakes.
caution.example: Screens should be used with caution.

boredom.definition: The feeling of being not interested in anything.
boredom.example: Boredom may be a necessary part of creativity.

component.definition: A necessary part of something larger.
component.example: Boredom may be a necessary component of creativity.

entertainments officer.definition: A person whose job is to keep people amused (used humorously here).
entertainments officer.example: You don't have to be the entertainments officer for your child.

confidence.definition: A feeling of belief in yourself and your abilities.
confidence.example: Tell your child you have confidence they will find something fun.

laid down.definition: Established or put in place at an early time.
laid down.example: What is laid down in childhood can stick for life.
